agent_output: {"executive_summary":"This architecture exhibits critical systemic risks that will cause cascading failures under load and severely limit operational agility. The single PostgreSQL instance with near-saturated connection pools creates a shared fate failure domain across all 8 microservices. Synchronous 4-hop call chains, a shared Redis session cache, monolithic deployments, and shared NFS logging negate nearly every benefit microservices are supposed to provide. This is a distributed monolith — it has the operational complexity of microservices with the coupling characteristics of a monolith.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway","responsibility":"Request routing, entry point for all client traffic","boundaries":"Unclear — likely also handling auth via shared Redis"},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management","boundaries":"Tightly coupled to Courier Service via synchronous HTTP"},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment and dispatch","boundaries":"Tightly coupled upstream to Order Service, downstream to Carrier API"},{"name":"Carrier API","responsibility":"External carrier integration","boundaries":"Synchronous dependency on external third-party systems — highest latency variance"},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"User notifications (email, push, SMS)","boundaries":"Sits at the tail of a 4-hop synchronous chain — failure here fails the entire order flow"},{"name":"Remaining 4 Services","responsibility":"Unknown — not specified","boundaries":"Share the same DB, Redis, deployment pipeline, and NFS mount as all others"},{"name":"PostgreSQL (Single Instance)","responsibility":"Primary data store for all 8 services","boundaries":"None — single shared instance, no schema or connection isolation"},{"name":"Redis (Shared Session Cache)","responsibility":"Session validation for every inbound request across all services","boundaries":"None — global shared state, single point of failure"}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Order Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Carrier API","contract":"Synchronous HTTP to external system","coupling_type":"temporal + platform"},{"from":"Carrier API","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral"},{"from":"All Services","to":"PostgreSQL","contract":"SQL via connection pool","coupling_type":"data + platform + shared fate"},{"from":"All Services","to":"Redis","contract":"Session lookup on every request","coupling_type":"temporal + shared fate"},{"from":"All Services","to":"NFS Mount","contract":"Log file writes","coupling_type":"platform + shared fate"}],"data_flow":"Every user request enters the API Gateway, hits Redis for session validation, then follows synchronous HTTP calls through up to 4 downstream services. Each service in the chain holds its database connection and HTTP connection open for the full duration of downstream processing. The critical order path is: Gateway → Order → Courier → Carrier API → Notification, meaning a single order request occupies resources across 5 services simultaneously. All services read/write to the same PostgreSQL instance and validate sessions against the same Redis instance.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Microservices (nominal)","rationale":"8 services exist, but shared DB, shared deployment, and synchronous coupling mean this is architecturally a distributed monolith"},{"pattern":"Synchronous Request-Response Chain","rationale":"All inter-service communication is synchronous HTTP — no async messaging, no event-driven patterns"},{"pattern":"Shared Database","rationale":"Single PostgreSQL instance used by all services — violates the database-per-service principle"}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","OrderLine"]},{"name":"Courier & Dispatch","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier","Assignment"]},{"name":"Carrier Integration","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CarrierRequest","ShipmentTracking"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationTemplate","DeliveryChannel"]},{"name":"Authentication/Session","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Session","UserToken"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier & Dispatch","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (but implemented as synchronous tight coupling — should be Async Event)"},{"upstream":"Courier & Dispatch","downstream":"Carrier Integration","relationship":"Conformist to external Carrier API (no ACL present)"},{"upstream":"Carrier Integration","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Direct synchronous call — should be domain event subscriber"},{"upstream":"Authentication/Session","downstream":"All Services","relationship":"Shared Kernel via Redis — all services conform to session schema"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single PostgreSQL instance shared by all services","gains":"Simple operations, single backup target, no data sync complexity, straightforward transactions","sacrifices":"Independent deployability, independent scalability, fault isolation, schema evolution freedom, connection pool headroom","alternatives_considered":["Database-per-service with eventual consistency","Shared instance but with per-service schemas and dedicated connection pool limits","Read replicas to offload read-heavy services"]},{"decision":"Synchronous HTTP for all inter-service communication","gains":"Simple mental model, easy debugging of individual calls, request-response semantics are familiar","sacrifices":"Availability (chain is only as available as its weakest link), latency (additive across hops), resilience (no buffering), resource holding (connections held open across full chain)","alternatives_considered":["Async messaging (RabbitMQ/Kafka) for non-critical path","CQRS with event sourcing for order state","Choreography-based saga for order fulfillment"]},{"decision":"Single CI/CD pipeline for all services","gains":"Simpler pipeline management, guaranteed version compatibility across services","sacrifices":"Deployment independence (the #1 reason to use microservices), blast radius (every deploy risks all 8 services), deployment frequency (slowest service gates all others), rollback granularity","alternatives_considered":["Per-service pipelines with contract testing","Monorepo with selective builds","Feature flags to decouple deploy from release"]},{"decision":"Shared Redis for session validation on every request","gains":"Consistent session state, simple auth model","sacrifices":"Redis becomes a global SPOF, every request adds Redis latency, Redis failure = total system outage","alternatives_considered":["JWT tokens with local validation (no network call)","Redis cluster with per-service read replicas","Session validation at gateway only, propagate claims downstream via signed tokens"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification Service","evolution_path":"Easiest service to decouple first — notifications are inherently asynchronous. Move to event-driven: Order/Courier publishes domain events, Notification subscribes. This breaks the synchronous chain from 4 hops to 3."},{"area":"Carrier Integration","evolution_path":"Wrap external Carrier API behind an Anti-Corruption Layer with circuit breaker and async queue. This isolates the highest-variance latency source from the critical path."},{"area":"Authentication","evolution_path":"Replace per-request Redis session lookup with JWT-based token validation at the gateway. Downstream services validate the signed token locally with zero network calls. Eliminates Redis as a shared-fate dependency."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Single PostgreSQL instance","reason":"Migrating from shared DB to database-per-service is the hardest architectural change. It requires decomposing schemas, handling distributed transactions, building data sync — this is a multi-quarter effort and must be planned carefully."},{"area":"Synchronous call chain coupling","reason":"Deeply embedded in all service-to-service interactions. Changing to async requires rethinking error handling, retry logic, idempotency, and user-facing response patterns across every service."},{"area":"Monolithic deployment pipeline","reason":"Splitting requires per-service CI/CD, contract testing, versioned APIs, and operational tooling for independent rollbacks. High investment but critical for operational maturity."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Connection pool exhaustion causing total system outage. 96 of 100 connections allocated. A single slow query, connection leak, or traffic spike in ANY service starves ALL services. PostgreSQL will reject connections, causing cascading failures across every microservice simultaneously.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediate: reduce per-service pool to 10 (80 total), reserving 20 connections for admin/monitoring. Short-term: add PgBouncer as a connection multiplexer. Medium-term: move to database-per-service or per-service schema with dedicated pool limits enforced at the proxy level."},{"risk":"Cascading failure through synchronous 4-hop call chain. If Carrier API (external, uncontrolled) slows to 10s response time, every upstream service holds connections open. Order Service threads exhaust, API Gateway backs up, all user requests fail — not just order-related ones.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediate: add circuit breakers (Hystrix/Resilience4j) at every synchronous boundary. Short-term: add timeouts with aggressive budgets (e.g., 2s total for the full chain, not 30s per hop). Medium-term: make Carrier API and Notification calls asynchronous."},{"risk":"Redis SPOF causes total authentication failure. Every request across all 8 services validates against a single Redis instance. Redis failure, network partition, or even high latency = every request fails auth = complete system outage.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediate: add Redis Sentinel or switch to Redis Cluster for HA. Short-term: add local session cache with short TTL (30s) as fallback. Medium-term: migrate to JWT tokens validated locally, eliminating the per-request Redis dependency entirely."},{"risk":"NFS mount failure causes log write blocking, which blocks request processing. If NFS mount becomes unavailable or slow, synchronous log writes in service threads will block, exhausting thread pools across all 8 services simultaneously.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Immediate: switch to async logging (write to local disk, ship via log agent like Fluentd/Filebeat). Never write application logs to a network filesystem synchronously."},{"risk":"Monolithic deployment creates all-or-nothing blast radius. A bug in the Notification Service takes down the Order Service, Courier Service, and all others because they deploy as a unit. Rollback affects all services even if only one is broken.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Short-term: implement feature flags to decouple deploy from release. Medium-term: split into per-service pipelines with contract testing and canary deployments."},{"risk":"No fault isolation between services at the data layer. A runaway query in one service (e.g., unindexed full table scan) locks tables or consumes all I/O, degrading all other services sharing the same PostgreSQL instance.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Immediate: implement per-service PostgreSQL roles with statement_timeout and connection limits. Add query monitoring (pg_stat_statements). Medium-term: schema-per-service with enforced resource limits."},{"risk":"Latency amplification under load. The synchronous 4-hop chain means tail latency is multiplicative. If each hop has p99 of 200ms, the chain p99 approaches 800ms+ (often worse due to queuing). Under load, this compounds with connection pool contention.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement timeout budgets that propagate: if the total budget is 2s, each downstream call gets the remaining budget minus processing time. Add deadline propagation headers."},{"risk":"Distributed monolith anti-pattern prevents independent scaling. The service with the highest traffic (likely API Gateway or Order Service) cannot be scaled independently because it shares the DB connection pool ceiling with all other services. Adding Order Service replicas steals connections from other services.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Connection pool limits must be managed as a global resource budget. PgBouncer with per-service pool limits prevents one service from starving others. Ultimately requires database-per-service."},{"risk":"Schema coupling through shared database. All services can read/write any table. A schema migration in one service's tables can break other services that have undocumented dependencies on those tables. No compile-time or deploy-time safety net.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Immediate: audit cross-service table access patterns. Enforce per-service schemas with restricted grants. Add schema migration testing that validates all services against proposed changes."},{"risk":"No backpressure mechanism anywhere in the system. Synchronous HTTP chains with shared connection pools and no circuit breakers mean the system has no way to shed load gracefully. Under stress, it will fail catastrophically rather than degrade gracefully.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Add rate limiting at the gateway, circuit breakers at every service boundary, and bulkhead patterns to isolate connection pools per downstream dependency."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Prioritized remediation in 4 phases. Phase 1 (Week 1-2, Critical): Add PgBouncer connection pooling proxy, implement circuit breakers and timeouts on all synchronous calls, switch to Redis Sentinel/Cluster, move logging off NFS to local disk + log shipper. Phase 2 (Week 3-6, High): Decouple Notification Service to async event-driven, add Anti-Corruption Layer around Carrier API with circuit breaker and retry queue, migrate auth to JWT with gateway-only validation. Phase 3 (Month 2-3, Medium): Split CI/CD into per-service pipelines with contract testing, implement per-service PostgreSQL schemas with restricted grants, add canary deployment capability. Phase 4 (Quarter 2-3, Strategic): Evaluate database-per-service migration for core services (Order, Courier), implement event-driven choreography for order fulfillment saga, add observability (distributed tracing, SLO-based alerting).","files_to_create_or_modify":["infrastructure/pgbouncer.ini — PgBouncer configuration with per-service pool limits","services/*/resilience.config — Circuit breaker and timeout configuration per service","infrastructure/redis-sentinel.conf — Redis HA configuration","infrastructure/fluentd/ — Log shipping configuration to replace NFS","services/api-gateway/auth/ — JWT validation middleware","services/notification-service/consumers/ — Event consumer replacing synchronous endpoint","infrastructure/ci/ — Per-service pipeline definitions","docs/adr/001-connection-pool-management.md","docs/adr/002-async-notification-decoupling.md","docs/adr/003-jwt-session-migration.md","docs/adr/004-database-per-service-roadmap.md"],"patterns_to_follow":"Circuit Breaker at every synchronous boundary. Bulkhead pattern for connection pool isolation. Timeout Budget propagation through call chains. Anti-Corruption Layer for external API integration. Event-Driven Architecture for non-critical-path operations (notifications, analytics). Strangler Fig pattern for incremental migration away from shared database. Gateway-level authentication with signed token propagation.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT increase PostgreSQL max_connections to solve pool exhaustion — this trades connection starvation for memory exhaustion and worse per-query performance","Do NOT add retry logic without idempotency — retries on non-idempotent operations cause duplicate orders, double charges, duplicate notifications","Do NOT split the database before splitting the deployment pipeline — you need independent deployability before independent data stores","Do NOT implement async messaging for the entire system at once — start with Notification Service (lowest risk, highest benefit) and learn operational patterns before expanding","Do NOT cache sessions locally without TTL and invalidation — stale sessions create security vulnerabilities","Do NOT treat PgBouncer as a permanent solution — it buys time but does not fix the fundamental shared-fate coupling at the data layer"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Connection pool exhaustion risk is mathematically certain under even modest load spikes (96/100 = 4 connections of headroom)","Synchronous chain cascading failure is a well-documented and inevitable failure mode","Redis SPOF and NFS logging risks are structural and verifiable from the architecture description","Monolithic deployment negating microservice benefits is a direct logical consequence"],"low_confidence_areas":["Actual traffic patterns and load distribution across the 8 services — remediation priority may shift if one service dominates traffic","Whether services actually share tables or already use logical schema separation — cross-service data coupling may be better or worse than assumed","The 4 unspecified services may have additional coupling patterns not captured in this audit","Whether the team has operational maturity for async messaging — this affects the feasibility timeline"],"assumptions":["PostgreSQL max_connections is set to 100 (as stated) with no PgBouncer or similar proxy in front","All 8 services connect directly to PostgreSQL, not through a connection multiplexer","Redis is a single instance, not a cluster or sentinel setup","NFS log writes are synchronous within the application thread","Services do not currently implement circuit breakers or timeout budgets","The Carrier API is an external third-party system outside the team's control"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"This architecture exhibits critical systemic risks that will cause cascading failures under load and severely limit operational agility. The single PostgreSQL instance with near-saturated connection pools creates a shared fate failure domain across all 8 microservices. Synchronous 4-hop call chains, a shared Redis session cache, monolithic deployments, and shared NFS logging negate nearly every benefit microservices are supposed to provide. This is a distributed monolith —

content.thinking: Let me analyze this architecture thoroughly and produce a comprehensive audit.

connection multiplexer","Redis is a single instance, not a cluster or sentinel setup","NFS log writes are synchronous within the application thread","Services do not currently implement circuit trace.result: {"executive_summary":"This architecture exhibits critical systemic risks that will cause cascading failures under load and severely limit operational agility. The single PostgreSQL instance with near-saturated connection pools creates a shared fate failure domain across all 8 microservices. Synchronous 4-hop call chains, a shared Redis session cache, monolithic deployments, and shared NFS logging negate nearly every benefit microservices are supposed to provide. This is a distributed monolith — it has the operational complexity of microservices with the coupling characteristics of a monolith.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway","responsibility":"Request routing, entry point for all client traffic","boundaries":"Unclear — likely also handling auth via shared Redis"},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management","boundaries":"Tightly coupled to Courier Service via synchronous HTTP"},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment and dispatch","boundaries":"Tightly coupled upstream to Order Service, downstream to Carrier API"},{"name":"Carrier API","responsibility":"External carrier integration","boundaries":"Synchronous dependency on external third-party systems — highest latency variance"},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"User notifications (email, push, SMS)","boundaries":"Sits at the tail of a 4-hop synchronous chain — failure here fails the entire order flow"},{"name":"Remaining 4 Services","responsibility":"Unknown — not specified","boundaries":"Share the same DB, Redis, deployment pipeline, and NFS mount as all others"},{"name":"PostgreSQL (Single Instance)","responsibility":"Primary data store for all 8 services","boundaries":"None — single shared instance, no schema or connection isolation"},{"name":"Redis (Shared Session Cache)","responsibility":"Session validation for every inbound request across all services","boundaries":"None — global shared state, single point of failure"}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Order Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Carrier API","contract":"Synchronous HTTP to external system","coupling_type":"temporal + platform"},{"from":"Carrier API","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP","coupling_type":"temporal + behavioral"},{"from":"All Services","to":"PostgreSQL","contract":"SQL via connection pool","coupling_type":"data + platform + shared fate"},{"from":"All Services","to":"Redis","contract":"Session lookup on every request","coupling_type":"temporal + shared fate"},{"from":"All Services","to":"NFS Mount","contract":"Log file writes","coupling_type":"platform + shared fate"}],"data_flow":"Every user request enters the API Gateway, hits Redis for session validation, then follows synchronous HTTP calls through up to 4 downstream services. Each service in the chain holds its database connection and HTTP connection open for the full duration of downstream processing. The critical order path is: Gateway → Order → Courier → Carrier API → Notification, meaning a single order request occupies resources across 5 services simultaneously. All services read/write to the same PostgreSQL instance and validate sessions against the same Redis instance.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Microservices (nominal)","rationale":"8 services exist, but shared DB, shared deployment, and synchronous coupling mean this is architecturally a distributed monolith"},{"pattern":"Synchronous Request-Response Chain","rationale":"All inter-service communication is synchronous HTTP — no async messaging, no event-driven patterns"},{"pattern":"Shared Database","rationale":"Single PostgreSQL instance used by all services — violates the database-per-service principle"}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","OrderLine"]},{"name":"Courier & Dispatch","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier","Assignment"]},{"name":"Carrier Integration","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CarrierRequest","ShipmentTracking"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationTemplate","DeliveryChannel"]},{"name":"Authentication/Session","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Session","UserToken"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier & Dispatch","relationship":"Customer-Supplier (but implemented as synchronous tight coupling — should be Async Event)"},{"upstream":"Courier & Dispatch","downstream":"Carrier Integration","relationship":"Conformist to external Carrier API (no ACL present)"},{"upstream":"Carrier Integration","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Direct synchronous call — should be domain event subscriber"},{"upstream":"Authentication/Session","downstream":"All Services","relationship":"Shared Kernel via Redis — all services conform to session schema"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single PostgreSQL instance shared by all services","gains":"Simple operations, single backup target, no data sync complexity, straightforward transactions","sacrifices":"Independent deployability, independent scalability, fault isolation, schema evolution freedom, connection pool headroom","alternatives_considered":["Database-per-service with eventual consistency","Shared instance but with per-service schemas and dedicated connection pool limits","Read replicas to offload read-heavy services"]},{"decision":"Synchronous HTTP for all inter-service communication","gains":"Simple mental model, easy debugging of individual calls, request-response semantics are familiar","sacrifices":"Availability (chain is only as available as its weakest link), latency (additive across hops), resilience (no buffering), resource holding (connections held open across full chain)","alternatives_considered":["Async messaging (RabbitMQ/Kafka) for non-critical path","CQRS with event sourcing for order state","Choreography-based saga for order fulfillment"]},{"decision":"Single CI/CD pipeline for all services","gains":"Simpler pipeline management, guaranteed version compatibility across services","sacrifices":"Deployment independence (the #1 reason to use microservices), blast radius (every deploy risks all 8 services), deployment frequency (slowest service gates all others), rollback granularity","alternatives_considered":["Per-service pipelines with contract testing","Monorepo with selective builds","Feature flags to decouple deploy from release"]},{"decision":"Shared Redis for session validation on every request","gains":"Consistent session state, simple auth model","sacrifices":"Redis becomes a global SPOF, every request adds Redis latency, Redis failure = total system outage","alternatives_considered":["JWT tokens with local validation (no network call)","Redis cluster with per-service read replicas","Session validation at gateway only, propagate claims downstream via signed tokens"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification Service","evolution_path":"Easiest service to decouple first — notifications are inherently asynchronous. Move to event-driven: Order/Courier publishes domain events, Notification subscribes. This breaks the synchronous chain from 4 hops to 3."},{"area":"Carrier Integration","evolution_path":"Wrap external Carrier API behind an Anti-Corruption Layer with circuit breaker and async queue. This isolates the highest-variance latency source from the critical path."},{"area":"Authentication","evolution_path":"Replace per-request Redis session lookup with JWT-based token validation at the gateway. Downstream services validate the signed token locally with zero network calls. Eliminates Redis as a shared-fate dependency."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Single PostgreSQL instance","reason":"Migrating from shared DB to database-per-service is the hardest architectural change. It requires decomposing schemas, handling distributed transactions, building data sync — this is a multi-quarter effort and must be planned carefully."},{"area":"Synchronous call chain coupling","reason":"Deeply embedded in all service-to-service interactions. Changing to async requires rethinking error handling, retry logic, idempotency, and user-facing response patterns across every service."},{"area":"Monolithic deployment pipeline","reason":"Splitting requires per-service CI/CD, contract testing, versioned APIs, and operational tooling for independent rollbacks. High investment but critical for operational maturity."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Connection pool exhaustion causing total system outage. 96 of 100 connections allocated. A single slow query, connection leak, or traffic spike in ANY service starves ALL services. PostgreSQL will reject connections, causing cascading failures across every microservice simultaneously.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediate: reduce per-service pool to 10 (80 total), reserving 20 connections for admin/monitoring. Short-term: add PgBouncer as a connection multiplexer. Medium-term: move to database-per-service or per-service schema with dedicated pool limits enforced at the proxy level."},{"risk":"Cascading failure through synchronous 4-hop call chain. If Carrier API (external, uncontrolled) slows to 10s response time, every upstream service holds connections open. Order Service threads exhaust, API Gateway backs up, all user requests fail — not just order-related ones.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediate: add circuit breakers (Hystrix/Resilience4j) at every synchronous boundary. Short-term: add timeouts with aggressive budgets (e.g., 2s total for the full chain, not 30s per hop). Medium-term: make Carrier API and Notification calls asynchronous."},{"risk":"Redis SPOF causes total authentication failure. Every request across all 8 services validates against a single Redis instance. Redis failure, network partition, or even high latency = every request fails auth = complete system outage.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediate: add Redis Sentinel or switch to Redis Cluster for HA. Short-term: add local session cache with short TTL (30s) as fallback. Medium-term: migrate to JWT tokens validated locally, eliminating the per-request Redis dependency entirely."},{"risk":"NFS mount failure causes log write blocking, which blocks request processing. If NFS mount becomes unavailable or slow, synchronous log writes in service threads will block, exhausting thread pools across all 8 services simultaneously.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Immediate: switch to async logging (write to local disk, ship via log agent like Fluentd/Filebeat). Never write application logs to a network filesystem synchronously."},{"risk":"Monolithic deployment creates all-or-nothing blast radius. A bug in the Notification Service takes down the Order Service, Courier Service, and all others because they deploy as a unit. Rollback affects all services even if only one is broken.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Short-term: implement feature flags to decouple deploy from release. Medium-term: split into per-service pipelines with contract testing and canary deployments."},{"risk":"No fault isolation between services at the data layer. A runaway query in one service (e.g., unindexed full table scan) locks tables or consumes all I/O, degrading all other services sharing the same PostgreSQL instance.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Immediate: implement per-service PostgreSQL roles with statement_timeout and connection limits. Add query monitoring (pg_stat_statements). Medium-term: schema-per-service with enforced resource limits."},{"risk":"Latency amplification under load. The synchronous 4-hop chain means tail latency is multiplicative. If each hop has p99 of 200ms, the chain p99 approaches 800ms+ (often worse due to queuing). Under load, this compounds with connection pool contention.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement timeout budgets that propagate: if the total budget is 2s, each downstream call gets the remaining budget minus processing time. Add deadline propagation headers."},{"risk":"Distributed monolith anti-pattern prevents independent scaling. The service with the highest traffic (likely API Gateway or Order Service) cannot be scaled independently because it shares the DB connection pool ceiling with all other services. Adding Order Service replicas steals connections from other services.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Connection pool limits must be managed as a global resource budget. PgBouncer with per-service pool limits prevents one service from starving others. Ultimately requires database-per-service."},{"risk":"Schema coupling through shared database. All services can read/write any table. A schema migration in one service's tables can break other services that have undocumented dependencies on those tables. No compile-time or deploy-time safety net.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Immediate: audit cross-service table access patterns. Enforce per-service schemas with restricted grants. Add schema migration testing that validates all services against proposed changes."},{"risk":"No backpressure mechanism anywhere in the system. Synchronous HTTP chains with shared connection pools and no circuit breakers mean the system has no way to shed load gracefully. Under stress, it will fail catastrophically rather than degrade gracefully.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Add rate limiting at the gateway, circuit breakers at every service boundary, and bulkhead patterns to isolate connection pools per downstream dependency."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Prioritized remediation in 4 phases. Phase 1 (Week 1-2, Critical): Add PgBouncer connection pooling proxy, implement circuit breakers and timeouts on all synchronous calls, switch to Redis Sentinel/Cluster, move logging off NFS to local disk + log shipper. Phase 2 (Week 3-6, High): Decouple Notification Service to async event-driven, add Anti-Corruption Layer around Carrier API with circuit breaker and retry queue, migrate auth to JWT with gateway-only validation. Phase 3 (Month 2-3, Medium): Split CI/CD into per-service pipelines with contract testing, implement per-service PostgreSQL schemas with restricted grants, add canary deployment capability. Phase 4 (Quarter 2-3, Strategic): Evaluate database-per-service migration for core services (Order, Courier), implement event-driven choreography for order fulfillment saga, add observability (distributed tracing, SLO-based alerting).","files_to_create_or_modify":["infrastructure/pgbouncer.ini — PgBouncer configuration with per-service pool limits","services/*/resilience.config — Circuit breaker and timeout configuration per service","infrastructure/redis-sentinel.conf — Redis HA configuration","infrastructure/fluentd/ — Log shipping configuration to replace NFS","services/api-gateway/auth/ — JWT validation middleware","services/notification-service/consumers/ — Event consumer replacing synchronous endpoint","infrastructure/ci/ — Per-service pipeline definitions","docs/adr/001-connection-pool-management.md","docs/adr/002-async-notification-decoupling.md","docs/adr/003-jwt-session-migration.md","docs/adr/004-database-per-service-roadmap.md"],"patterns_to_follow":"Circuit Breaker at every synchronous boundary. Bulkhead pattern for connection pool isolation. Timeout Budget propagation through call chains. Anti-Corruption Layer for external API integration. Event-Driven Architecture for non-critical-path operations (notifications, analytics). Strangler Fig pattern for incremental migration away from shared database. Gateway-level authentication with signed token propagation.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT increase PostgreSQL max_connections to solve pool exhaustion — this trades connection starvation for memory exhaustion and worse per-query performance","Do NOT add retry logic without idempotency — retries on non-idempotent operations cause duplicate orders, double charges, duplicate notifications","Do NOT split the database before splitting the deployment pipeline — you need independent deployability before independent data stores","Do NOT implement async messaging for the entire system at once — start with Notification Service (lowest risk, highest benefit) and learn operational patterns before expanding","Do NOT cache sessions locally without TTL and invalidation — stale sessions create security vulnerabilities","Do NOT treat PgBouncer as a permanent solution — it buys time but does not fix the fundamental shared-fate coupling at the data layer"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Connection pool exhaustion risk is mathematically certain under even modest load spikes (96/100 = 4 connections of headroom)","Synchronous chain cascading failure is a well-documented and inevitable failure mode","Redis SPOF and NFS logging risks are structural and verifiable from the architecture description","Monolithic deployment negating microservice benefits is a direct logical consequence"],"low_confidence_areas":["Actual traffic patterns and load distribution across the 8 services — remediation priority may shift if one service dominates traffic","Whether services actually share tables or already use logical schema separation — cross-service data coupling may be better or worse than assumed","The 4 unspecified services may have additional coupling patterns not captured in this audit","Whether the team has operational maturity for async messaging — this affects the feasibility timeline"],"assumptions":["PostgreSQL max_connections is set to 100 (as stated) with no PgBouncer or similar proxy in front","All 8 services connect directly to PostgreSQL, not through a connection multiplexer","Redis is a single instance, not a cluster or sentinel setup","NFS log writes are synchronous within the application thread","Services do not currently implement circuit breakers or timeout budgets","The Carrier API is an external third-party system outside the team's control"]}}
